annotations.label: Harmonic analysis

anchor.snippet: Harmonic analysis is an area of mathematical analysis that emerged from the study of harmonic functions

annotations.note: A descriptive overview of a field, not a formal mathematical statement to formalize.

annotations.label: Abstract harmonic analysis (tradition)

anchor.snippet: A related tradition is abstract harmonic analysis where the emphasis is on functions and representations on topological groups

annotations.note: A description of a research tradition; no single formal counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Calderón–Zygmund decomposition

anchor.snippet: One example is the Calderón–Zygmund decomposition

annotations.note: No occurrence of Calderón–Zygmund decomposition found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hilbert transform weak-type bound

anchor.snippet: For example, the Hilbert transform is not bounded in

annotations.note: The Hilbert transform itself is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fourier restriction problem

anchor.snippet: A restriction problem asks the question of whether the Fourier transform of a function can be meaningfully restricted to a lower-dimensional set in frequency space

annotations.note: No Fourier restriction operator or problem formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extension operator

anchor.snippet: Equivalently, by duality, one studies the extension operator

annotations.note: The Fourier extension operator dual to restriction is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stein–Tomas theorem

anchor.snippet: A basic result of this type is the Stein–Tomas theorem

annotations.note: No Stein–Tomas theorem found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lebesgue differentiation theorem

anchor.snippet: One is the Lebesgue differentiation theorem , which states that a locally integrable function is almost everywhere equal to the limit of its average over balls

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Lebesgue differentiation theorem via Vitali families, with `ae_tendsto_average_norm_sub` and `ae_tendsto_average` stated as such.

annotations.label: Maximal ergodic theorem

anchor.snippet: The maximal ergodic theorem is analogous to the Hardy–Littlewood maximal theorem

annotations.note: No maximal ergodic theorem (nor Hardy–Littlewood maximal function) found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Birkhoff ergodic theorem

anchor.snippet: Such maximal estimates are used in proofs of the Birkhoff ergodic theorem

annotations.note: Birkhoff averages are defined and the Von Neumann mean ergodic theorem is proved, but the pointwise Birkhoff ergodic theorem itself is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Calderón transference principle

anchor.snippet: Calderón showed that harmonic analytic estimates for translation-invariant operators can be transferred to ergodic averages

annotations.note: No Calderón transference principle found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Spherical harmonic decomposition

anchor.snippet: An example is the decomposition of a function on Euclidean space into spherical harmonics

annotations.note: Spherical harmonics and their L² decomposition are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pontryagin duality

anchor.snippet: One of the major results in the theory of functions on abelian locally compact groups is called Pontryagin duality

annotations.note: `PontryaginDual` is defined for topological groups, and the double-dual isomorphism is proved only for finite abelian groups; the general LCA Pontryagin duality theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Peter–Weyl theorem

anchor.snippet: For compact groups, the Peter–Weyl theorem explains how one may get harmonics by choosing one irreducible representation out of each equivalence class of representations

annotations.note: No Peter–Weyl theorem found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tate's thesis

anchor.snippet: In Tate's thesis , questions about L-functions are formulated using harmonic analysis on the adele ring and idele group

annotations.note: Adele rings of number fields are defined as infrastructure, but Tate's thesis itself (local/global functional equation via adelic Fourier analysis) is not formalized.
